name: File Revision Intent
summary: UX-only protocol for the distinguishing trait of a file-UPDATE control over a plain upload: a value already exists, so the control owns revert-to-original and the moment the staged replacement becomes the displayed value. Models presence as an axis (valuePresence: existing vs empty) so file-upload and file-update are the SAME control at two settings — the droplist-style answer to 'is update a distinct standard or just upload's under-modeled current-value dimension'. Entry/accept (file-source, drop-target, accepts) is the data-transfer intent's territory (#007); transfer/commit/persistence are technical and live in the File Upload & Update configurator domain, not here. INITIAL vocabulary, pending owner review.

valuePresence.description: Whether a current value already exists when the control mounts — the single axis that separates UPDATE from UPLOAD. 'existing' unlocks revert and the swap model below; 'empty' degrades the same control to a plain upload with no original to preserve. Modeling it as a dimension is the claim that upload and update are one component, not two.

swapModel.description: When the displayed value flips from the current file to the staged replacement. 'confirmed' (default) keeps showing the original until the commit succeeds — the data-safe choice when an existing value is precious; 'optimistic' shows the replacement immediately and rolls back to the original on failure. UX-only: where the bytes actually go (endpoint / OPFS / File System Access handle / form value) is the technical commit strategy, handled in the configurator.

revert.description: Whether restoring the original value is offered while a replacement is staged (the affordance a greenfield upload has no concept of). 'enabled' (default) lets the user back out to the pristine value before committing; 'disabled' makes the replacement one-way once staged. Pairs with the observable revision state pristine → staged → committed that drives both the control's affordances and its live-region announcements.

description:
What File Revision Is

A file update control is not a new widget — it is a plain file upload that starts with a current value. That one precondition is the whole intent: because a value already exists, the control owns semantics a greenfield upload never needs — revert-to-original and a deliberate choice about when the staged replacement becomes the displayed value. File Revision names exactly that delta and nothing else, so a bare <input type="file">, a drag-and-drop dropzone, and a resumable chunked uploader are the same control at different trait altitudes (the droplist decomposition, applied to files).

Presence Is the Axis

The open question 'is file-update a distinct standard, or just the observation that the upload component always had an under-modeled current-value dimension?' is answered by making presence a dimension: valuePresence: existing is an update, empty is an upload, and they are one component. existing is what unlocks revert and the swap model; empty degrades cleanly to the platform default.

Scope — UX Only

Per the intent rule, this models the user-facing concern only. The technical axes a real upload needs are deliberately not dimensions here:

  • How files enter (native picker / dropzone / paste / directory / File System Access handle) and what is accepted (type / size / count) are the data-transfer intent's drop-target / accepts contract (#007) — File Revision composes it, never redefines it.
  • How bytes move (single / chunked / resumable) and where they are written (POST/PUT endpoint, OPFS, FS handle, form value) are technical strategies, operationalized as the File Upload & Update domain in the Technical Configurator — not intent vocabulary.
  • Resumability (the IETF Resumable Uploads draft / the de-facto tus protocol) maps onto a Reliability recovery handler (resume = retry-from-offset), so it belongs there rather than in a bespoke transfer trait.

Composition

File Revision composes, rather than re-specifies: the data-transfer intent (#007) for the source affordance and accept-constraint; the Validation intent for pre-transfer gating of type/size/count; the Loader / Background Task intents for the idle → selected → transferring → done | error progress lifecycle and its live-region announcements; and Reliability for resumable transfer. Its own contribution is purely the revision delta below.

Native Substrate

The always-keyboard-operable baseline is <input type="file"> (with accept, multiple, capture, webkitdirectory); the File API (File/Blob/FileList) carries the bytes. A bare input has no notion of a current value, so the revision state (pristine vs staged) is the component's to track — but every higher trait must degrade back to that operable input, and progress-state changes must announce via a live region. This intent mandates the a11y floor; it does not bolt it on.

Interface Protocol

type ValuePresence = 'existing' | 'empty';
type SwapModel = 'confirmed' | 'optimistic';
type RevisionState = 'pristine' | 'staged' | 'committed';

interface FileRevisionIntent {
    // Does a current value exist at mount? 'existing' = update, 'empty' = upload.
    valuePresence: ValuePresence;
    // When the staged replacement becomes the displayed value.
    swapModel: SwapModel;          // default 'confirmed'
    // Offer restore-to-original while a replacement is staged.
    revert: boolean;               // default true
    // Observable revision lifecycle — drives affordances + announcements.
    readonly state: RevisionState;
    // Source affordance + accept-constraint are composed from the data-transfer intent (#007),
    // not owned here; transfer + commit strategy are technical (see the configurator).
}
